coredata.dc:title: Printable Thermo- and Photo-stable Poly(D,L-lactide)/Carbon Nanodots Nanocomposites via Heterophase Melt-Extrusion Transesterification
coredata.dc:creator: Mauro, Nicolò
Andrea Utzeri, Mara
Sciortino, Alice
Cannas, Marco
Messina, Fabrizio
Cavallaro, Gennara
Giammona, Gaetano
coredata.dc:description: We propose for the first time an one-pot synthesis of carbon nanodots-poly(D,L-lactide) (CDs-PLA) nanocomposites, obtained by a simple reactive melt-extrusion process involving polar surface groups of multicolor CDs and ester bonds of PLA chains. Apart from providing an excellent method to produce polyester-coated CDs, our protocol allows obtaining perfect PLA@CDs blends giving rise to homogeneous extruded PLA@CDs filaments (ePLA@CDs) suitable for 3D printing applications (e.g., additive manufacturing for biomaterials and biodegradable encoded polymer ink technology). We demonstrate that ePLA@CDs filaments can be used to build a huge range of fluorescent objects with increasing architectural complexity (from grids to orthopedic screws). The designed nanocomposite synergistically combines the brilliant optical properties of hydrophilic N,S-CDs with the known biodegradable and biocompatible properties of PLA. Indeed, CDs endow PLA with peculiar thermo- and photo-stable multicolor emission (from blue to NIR) as well as good printability, avoiding typical self-quenching phenomena of CDs in solid-state. Besides, the use of CDs as probe-filler assumes a multifunctional role improving water uptake, degradation rate and also increasing material-cell interactions in comparison with the plain PLA. Overall, our results pave the way for the design of fluorescent implants providing real-time localization and monitoring of degradation state via non-invasive fluorescence imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyesters
PLA
Melt transesterification
Multicolor carbon nanodots
Fluorescent scaffolds
3D printing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemical characterization of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite by 1H NMR
Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA)
Optical characterization of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite
Degradation study of PLA and ePLA@CDs
Preparation of ePLA@CDs and PLA films for biological characterizations
3-D printed fluorescent biomaterials: A proof-of-concept study
Synthesis of extruded PLA@CDs nanocomposite (ePLA@CDs) by heterophase melt-extrusion transesterification
Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of ePLA@CDs
Preparation and microstructure characterization of ePLA@CDs filaments for 3D printing
Degradation study of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite
Study of cell adhesion and proliferation on PLA@CDs films
3-D printing of self-tracking biomaterials: A proof-of-concept study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic scheme adopted for ePLA@CDs by heterophase melt-extrusion transesterification.

FT-IR spectra of pure PLA, CDs and extruded PLA@CDs (ePLA@CDs) (a). Zoom of PLA and ePLA@CDs FT-IR spectra (a'). FT-IR spectra of PLA@CDs pre-extrusion and ePLA@CDs (a’’). Table of functional groups detected by FT-IR analysis of PLA (b). Size exclusion chromatography (SEC) of ePLA@CDs and and pure PLA as control (c). 1H NMR (300 MHz, [D1]CHCl3) of purified PLA-CDs conjugates obtained after free PLA removal by column chromatography (d).
DSC (a-a’) and TGA (a’’) thermograms obtained for CDs, ePLA@CDs and PLA. X-ray micro-CT analysis of extruded filament of ePLA@CDs (b-b’’’). Water contact angle measurements of PLA and ePLA@CDs (c).

Absorbance and emissive profile at different excitation wavelength (440, 470, 500, 530 nm) of bare N,S-CDs (a); emission spectra obtained at increasing excitation wavelength (440, 470, 500, 530 nm) of ePLA@CDs (b); fluorescence spectra of bare N,S-CDs (red curves) and ePLA@CDs (black curves) exited at 440 and 530 nm (c); fluorescence decay kinetics of bare N,S-CDs (black curve) and ePLA@CDs (red curve) (d); blue emission of a N,S-CDs dispersion in water (e); blue emission of a printed grid from ePLA@CDs (f).

Degradation study for ePLA@CDs and plain PLA filaments in PBS pH 7.4. Water uptake (a). X-ray Micro-CT of a plain PLA filament (b-b') and a ePLA@CDs filmanent (c-c''') after 31 weeks of incubation. Fluorescence microscopy images of a ePLA@CDs filmanent after 31 weeks of incubation: trasversal section (d-d’’’) and longitudinal surface (e-e''') view.
Cell viability of MC3T3 and NHDF on PLA@CDs and plain PLA films after 24 h and 48 h of incubation. (*p-value < 0.05, **p-value < 0.01).

ePLA@CDs 3D printed model of a circle grid (a) and a dodecagon ring (a’-a’’). X-ray micro-CT analysis of 3D printed screw model of ePLA@CDs from the bottom view (b) and cross-section (b’). Fluorescence microscopy images of printed ePLA@CDs grid (c).

head.ce:title: Printable Thermo- and Photo-stable Poly(D,L-lactide)/Carbon Nanodots Nanocomposites via Heterophase Melt-Extrusion Transesterification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lab of Biocompatible Polymers, Department of Biological, Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences and Technologies (STEBICEF), University of Palermo, via Archirafi 32, 90123 Palermo, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biological, Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences and Technologies (STEBICEF)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Lab of Biocompatible Polymers, Department of Biological, Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences and Technologies (STEBICEF), University of Palermo, via Archirafi 32, 90123 Palermo, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Chemistry (DiFC) “E. Segrè”, University of Palermo, via Archirafi 36, 90123 Palermo, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Chemistry (DiFC) “E. Segrè”

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Chemistry (DiFC) “E. Segrè”, University of Palermo, via Archirafi 36, 90123 Palermo, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ATeN Center - Advanced Technologies Network Center, University of Palermo, Viale delle Scienze - Edificio 18/A, 90128 Palermo, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: ATeN Center - Advanced Technologies Network Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: ATeN Center - Advanced Technologies Network Center, University of Palermo, Viale delle Scienze - Edificio 18/A, 90128 Palermo, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We propose for the first time an one-pot synthesis of carbon nanodots-poly(D,L-lactide) (CDs-PLA) nanocomposites, obtained by a simple reactive melt-extrusion process involving polar surface groups of multicolor CDs and ester bonds of PLA chains. Apart from providing an excellent method to produce polyester-coated CDs, our protocol allows obtaining perfect PLA@CDs blends giving rise to homogeneous extruded PLA@CDs filaments (ePLA@CDs) suitable for 3D printing applications (e.g., additive manufacturing for biomaterials and biodegradable encoded polymer ink technology). We demonstrate that ePLA@CDs filaments can be used to build a huge range of fluorescent objects with increasing architectural complexity (from grids to orthopedic screws). The designed nanocomposite synergistically combines the brilliant optical properties of hydrophilic N,S-CDs with the known biodegradable and biocompatible properties of PLA. Indeed, CDs endow PLA with peculiar thermo- and photo-stable multicolor emission (from blue to NIR) as well as good printability, avoiding typical self-quenching phenomena of CDs in solid-state. Besides, the use of CDs as probe-filler assumes a multifunctional role improving water uptake, degradation rate and also increasing material-cell interactions in comparison with the plain PLA. Overall, our results pave the way for the design of fluorescent implants providing real-time localization and monitoring of degradation state via non-invasive fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the design of hybrid biomaterials has aroused considerable interest as a valid strategy to synthesize innovative biodegradable nanocomposites with specific and improved properties compared to individual components [1]. Particularly, the introduction of inorganic nano-fillers into biodegradable polymeric matrices such as poly(D,L-lactide) (PLA) can impart superior structural and functional features, allowing to overcome common limitations of conventional polymeric biomaterials for tissue engineering [2]. In particular, the synthesis of self-tracked nanocomposites has gained great interest, especially in the development of 3D printed implantable medical devices, due to the possibility of monitoring their localization and degradation rate in real-time by non-invasive fluorescence imaging techniques [3,4]. Many inorganic and organic probes have been used so far [5–9]. However, they usually suffer from thermal and optical instability, high costs, and toxicity which have barred from any chance of allowing 3D printing of thermoplastic biodegradable plastics [10–12].

In this context, due to their brilliant multicolor fluorescence, resistance to photobleaching and biocompatibility [13–15], carbon nanodots (CDs) emerge as an effective fluorescent nanomaterial exploitable as probe-filler of biodegradable polymeric matrices such as polyesters. CDs are an attractive alternative to conventional dyes and metal nanoparticles, whose application on self-tracked biodevice production is limited because of rapid photobleaching and higher cytotoxicity, respectively [16,17]. However, one of the main drawbacks on developing efficient fluorescent CDs-based polymeric devices is the self-quenching induced by π-π stacking interactions or aggregation phenomena in solid-state, together with the scarce reaction yields reported for most CDs. In fact, as a rule, CDs are usually obtained with yields lower than 5% w/w, and one often observes an enhancement of non-radiative mechanisms and turning off the emissive properties of CDs in solid dispersions which have excluded bulk applications for CDs [18–20]. Therefore, the development of new synthetic approach to obtain emissive CDs in solid-state, with high yield, is of particular interest.

On these grounds, we have very recently developed an efficient protocol to obtain decagram-scale quantities of N,S-doped CDs with a narrow size distribution, along with a single nanostructure multicolor emission (blue-to-near infrared-NIR), and high NIR photothermal conversion efficiency. They have proved capable of acting as a biocompatible self-tracking nano-heater to photothermally eradicate only cancer cells in vitro under the guidance of fluorescence imaging (LFI) [21]. We proposed for the first time a novel one-pot synthesis of poly(D,L-lactide) conjugates, henceforth named CDs-PLA, by a melt-extrusion transesterification method yielding homogeneous nanocomposites consisting of CDs-PLA embedded in a matrix of PLA. Melt-extrusion transesterification is already described for polyester mixtures in order to produce cost-effective copolymers with tunable mechanical and chemical properties [22–24] Indeed, extrusion processes represent an interesting cost-effective tool to manufacture biodegradable polymers like PLA, and to induce diverse solvent-free chemical reactions in presence or not of catalysts (e.g. ester copolymerization, grafting and functionalizations) [25,26]. However, to the best of our knowledge this is the first time that this method is proposed to obtain surface functionalized amphiphilic carbon nanoparticles which lead to highly homogeneous nanocomposite polyester blends with self-tracking capabilities. Unlike other reports, where CDs were used to produce printable fluorescent physical blends, herein, CDs are covalently linked to PLA chains, thus obtaining completely dispersed probe-fillers without direct dot-to-dot interactions. We demonstrated that extrusion can be a valid general strategy for the one-pot synthesis of nanocomposite PLA@CDs filaments useful to print multicolor fluorescent biomaterials with high structural complexity and excellent photo- and thermal stability. We studied how CDs impinge on the main characteristics of PLA, one of the most widely used biomaterials in clinic [27,28], and on its biological performance. Overall, we explored how the use of CDs as probe-filler affect the features of PLA in term of printability, interactions at the material-cell interface, water uptake capacity and degradation rate, evaluating the possibility to exploit the hydrophilic character of CDs to tune the degradation kinetic of PLA as a function of clinical needs and applications.

Urea (99%), citric acid (99.5%), anhydrous N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Milan, Italy). Indocyanine green (ICG, 99.5%), Sephadex® G15, Sephadex® G25, dialysis tubing MWCO 2 kDa, poly(D,L-lactide) acid terminated (Mw 14 kDa), chloroform, dichloromethane (DCM), phosphate buffer saline (PBS) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Milan, Italy).

Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), L-glutamine, penicillin, streptomycin and amphotericin B were purchased from EuroClone (Milan, Italy). CellTiter 96® AQueous One Solution Cell Proliferation Assay (MTS) was purchased from Promega (Milan, Italy).

Size exclusion chromatography (SEC) traces were obtained using an Agilent 1260 Infinity equipped with Yarra™ 3 µm columns connected in series to right angle (RA) and low angle (LA) light scattering (LS) detectors, and a refractive index (RI) detector. The mobile phase was 0.01 M LiBr in DMF: flow 0.8 mL min−1, temperature. The flow rate was 1 mL min−1.

Carbon nanodots were synthesized via solvothermal synthesis from urea (11.56 g, 0.0577 mol), citric acid (36.95 g, 0.0577 mol) and indocyanine green (100 mg, 0.129 mmol) in anhydrous DMF (100 mL) as previously reported [21]. The reaction was kept at 170 °C for 6 h under controlled pressure conditions, thanks to the use of an autoclave (Büchi AG, Miniclave steel type 3, Gschwaderstrasse, Switzerland). Pure N,S-CDs were obtained as a brownish powder after solvent evaporation and purification by size exclusion chromatography (SEC) using a column packed in turn with Sephadex® G15 and G25. Yield 43%.

CDs (100 mg, 2.98 mg mL−1) were dispersed in acetone/water (95:5) by sonicating, and the colloidal dispersion was added to a homogeneous solution of PLA in chloroform (20% w/v, 100 mL) dropwise, obtaining a clear PLA@CDs dispersion. Then, the dark orange blend was kept under stirring overnight at room temperature. Successively, about 30 mL of organic solvent was removed by rotary evaporator, obtaining a slurry which was distributed on a glass crystallizer to slowly remove solvents at room temperature overnight. The obtained brown PLA@CDs film was cut into small chops. Successively, PLA@CDs chops (20 g) were introduced into the extruder at barrel section (Noztek Pro Filament Extruder – UK), and extruded at 185 °C to give rise filaments of ePLA@CDs of 1.75 ± 0.05 mm diameter.

FTIR-ATR analysis of PLA, CDs, PLA@CDs and ePLA@CDs were performed to investigate interactions established between PLA chains and the CDs surface before and after extrusion. FTIR-ATR measurements were carried out using a Bruker Alpha II spectrometer, recording the spectra in the range between 400 and 4000 cm−1. CDs were analysed as dry powder, while PLA, PLA@CDs and ePLA@CDs samples were prepared as films by solvent casting method from a dispersion in chloroform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical characterization of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite by 1H NMR
ce:section.ce:para: To attain the chemical composition and structural features of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite, ePLA@CDs was purified by gel permeation chromatography (GPC) using a column packed with Sephadex® LH-20 resin and DCM as eluent. In particular, ePLA@CDs (500 mg) was solubilized in DCM (10 mL), and the solution was purified by GPC (flow 0.2 mL min−1) in order to isolate only carbon nanodots present in the blend. Colorless fractions of pure PLA and brownish fractions of CDs-PLA conjugates were collected and analyzed by 1H NMR (Bruker 300 MHz) in CDCl3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA)
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal stability of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite and the nature of interactions taking place between CDs and PLA were evaluated by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) coupled with thermogravimetric analysis (DSC/TGA). Pure CDs and PLA were analysed as controls. The analyses were performed using a DSC/TGA 131 EVO (by SETARAM Instr.) under nitrogen flow (1 mL min−1), and using aluminium crucibles. The heating rate was: 30–600 °C, 10 °C min−1.

Surface wettability of ePLA@CDs was evaluated in comparison with pure PLA films by contact angle measurements carried out using a DSA25 by Kruss Germany. Contact angle was measured after the deposition of a water drop on either ePLA@CDs or PLA solvent-cast films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The optical absorption measurements were collected in a 1 cm quartz cuvette by a Star Line ULS2048CLEVO single-beam optical fiber spectrophotometer (Avantes) equipped with a dual halogen-deuterium light source in a spectral range of 200–900 nm. Steady-state emission spectra were recorded on an intensified CCD camera integrating the signal during temporal windows of 1 ms from the excitation laser pulse. A tunable laser (OPO – optical parametric oscillator) providing 5 ns pulses at 10 Hz has been employed to excite the samples. Time-resolved measurements were recorded in the same setup integrating the signal in a shorter temporal window which lasts 0.5 ns and varying the delay from the laser pulse. The accuracy of the time constant of the decay is about 0.2 ns.

Quantum yield (QY) values were estimated by measuring the emission and absorption of the sample in an integrating sphere. The samples have been excited by laser diodes peaking at 405 nm and at 532 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation study of PLA and ePLA@CDs

ce:section.ce:para:
Percentage water uptake of extruded filaments of ePLA@CDs (1.7 ± 0.1 cm × 1.5 ± 0.5 mm) and pure PLA were determined in PBS pH 7.4 (15 mL) at 37 °C up to 31 weeks. At schedules time intervals samples were retrieved, wiped-off with soft paper and weighed. Water uptake was evaluated using the following equation:
(1)
WU % =
Wf - W i
Wi
× 100
Where Wi and Wf are the initial and final weisght, respectively. Each value is a mean of six measures obtained using six different samples.

Structural defects in ePLA@CDs and PLA filaments were studied by Micro-CT acquisitions using a Bruker Skyscan 1272 both before and after their degradation under physiological conditions (pH 7.4, 37 °C). In particular, after 24 days of incubation in PBS pH 7.4 at 37 °C, filaments were withdrawn, wiped-off and analyzed. Measurements were performed at a voltage of 40 kW and a current of 250 µA with a rotation step of 0.2° and a resolution of 4.5 µm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ePLA@CDs and PLA films for biological characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: ePLA@CDs and PLA films were obtained by the solvent casting method. Briefly, ePLA@CDs or pure PLA was dissolved in DCM by magnetic agitation (50 mg mL−1). Successively, the solution was transferred to petri dished and dried at room temperature overnight until constant weight. The resulting films were washed up with sterile DPBS (10 mL) for two hours and sterilized by immersion in ethanol/water 70:30 for 5 min before using.

Cell adhesion on PLA and ePLA@CDs films were monitored after 24 h and 48 h of incubation using normal pre-osteoblast (MC3T3) and normal human dermal fibroblast (NHDF) by MTS assay. Before incubation, films were washed three-times with sterile Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffered saline (DPBS) to remove ethanol traces. ePLA@CDs and PLA films were placed in a 48-multiwell plate by using plate inserts (CellCrownTM48, Sigma-Aldrich), and then cells were seeded on films (5 × 104 cells per well). After 1 h of incubation at 37 °C and 5% of CO2, each well was filled with DMEM (400 µL) to permit cells to adhere on films. After 24 h and 48 h, each film was withdrawn, washed twice with DPBS pH 7.4 and transferred in a new 48-multiwell plate before performing the MTS assay. Then, a MTS solution in DMEM (400 µL) was added to each well, and cells were incubated for additional 3 h at 37 °C. Successively, the absorbance was measured at 492 nm by using a microplate reader (Multiskan, Thermo, U.K.). All biological experiments were performed in triplicate and results are reported as mean values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3-D printed fluorescent biomaterials: A proof-of-concept study
ce:section.ce:para: The printability of ePLA@CDs filaments was explored using 3D printed patterns designed through Voxelizer software to generate a set of G-code for 3D printing. The models were printed in a layer-by-layer manner by using a Z morph 3D Printer with a heated single extrusion head (1.75 mm diameter) at 185 °C and a deposition of melted ePLA@CDs filament through a nozzle of 0.3 mm on a collection surface at 60 °C. The microstructural characterization was attained by Micro-CT acquisitions using a Bruker Skyscan 1272 as above described for filaments. Samples was fixed to the support using wax for the microscopy examination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of extruded PLA@CDs nanocomposite (ePLA@CDs) by heterophase melt-extrusion transesterification
ce:section.ce:para: N,S-co-doped carbon nanodots (d = 5.3 ± 0.4 nm) with multicolor tunable emission (N,S-CDs) were synthetized by solvothermal decomposition of urea, citric acid and indocyanine green (ICG) in DMF, as previously described (Figure S1a-b)[21]. Briefly, a controlled nitrogen- sulfur- co-doped carbon nanocore was synthesized by using equimolar amount of citric acid and urea, while a non-stoichiometric amount of ICG was used as sulfur donor. This strategy allowed to improve CDs optical properties in term of multicolor fluorescence (from green-QY 6% to NIR-QY 1%) and photothermal conversion capacity (46 ± 4%), and provides CDs bearing different surface polar groups amenable to further surface functionalization, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, amide and sulfonate/sulfoxide (Figure S1c), and a crystalline core (Figure S2). In particular, hydroxyl groups at the N,S-CDs surface could be in principle exploited to provoke one-pot solid/liquid heterophase transesterification of polyesters (e.g., PLA, PCL and PLGA) by melt-extrusion processes (Fig. 1 )[29].

The ePLA@CDs nanocomposite was designed to combine the thermoplastic features of PLA, which imply versatile printability to build up 3D biocompatible and biodegradable biomaterials for several biomedical applications, and the multicolor fluorescence of water soluble and biocompatible N,S-CDs that can provide smart self-tracking capabilities. However, the high interfacial energy between polar CDs and non-polar polyesters such as PLA precludes the formation of homogeneous physical blends, while hydrophobic CDs cannot be used because of their poor optical and biological properties. Thus, obtaining a perfect blend is not trivial. As represented in Fig. 1, to obtain PLA@CDs nanocomposites a dispersion of crystalline N,S-CDs in acetone was slowly added dropwise into a 20% PLA solution, obtaining a homogeneous PLA@CDs dispersion with the 0.1% w/w of CDs. The amount of CDs in the preparation was chosen to allow obtaining an uniform colloidal distribution of CDs into the PLA matrix, free of aggregates and precipitate, thus providing homogeneous PLA@CDs chops after the solvent casting procedure. Besides, preliminary experiments showed us that increasing the amount of CDs beyond 0.1% w/w provides dot-to-dot interactions responsible for fluorescence quenching in CDs (Figure S3). PLA@CDs chops obtained by solvent-casting were extruded as filaments at 185 °C. Unlike the very recently reported extruded hybrid PLA/CDs nanocomposite, in which the extrusion process was used to allow mixing solid components in a physical blend [30], here, the principle of the heterophase melt-extrusion transesterification process was exploited to induce direct transesterification between the hydroxyl groups at the CDs surface and the ester bonds of PLA chains. This route yielded CDs-PLA conjugates embedded into the hydrophobic matrix of unbounded PLA chains, leading to a highly homogeneous nanocomposite material with thermoplastic features.

The formation of homogeneous nanocomposites was preliminary studied by FTIR of ePLA@CDs films compared to starting CDs and PLA (Fig. 2 a-a’’). As shown in Fig. 2a, N,S-CDs are characterized by several vibration bands relative to hydroxyl (3420 cm−1), amine (3200 cm−1), amide (amide I band, 1640 cm−1), carboxyl (asymmetric stretching at 1715 cm−1), and sulfonate/sulfoxide groups (1391 and 1283 cm−1). While ePLA@CDs spectrum resembles characteristic bands of PLA spectrum reported in Fig. 2b. As shown in Fig. 2a’, in the ePLA@CDs spectrum the intensity ratios I1129/I1083 and I1041/I1083 are higher than that observed for the PLA spectrum. Moreover, peaks within the fingerprint region (1129 and 1083 cm−1) do not show the same symmetry displayed in the PLA spectrum, suggesting an increment of the OH bending. It is also important to notice that the peak at 753 cm−1 in the ePLA@CDs spectrum splits into two peaks centered at 753–734 cm−1, hinting at different O-C = O/OH ratios which implies higher OH bending vibrations in the nanocomposite spectrum attributable to transesterification processes taking place during extrusion. This hypothesis is also supported by substantial differences observed between ePLA@CDs and non-extruded PLA@CDs spectra (Fig. 2a’’), where symmetric peaks at 1129–1083 cm−1 and a single peak more intense at 753 cm−1 can be observed for PLA@CDs. Thus, this suggests melt-extrusion transesterification between ester bonds of PLA and hydroxyl groups of CDs, with the consequent formation of PLA-coated CDs (CDs-PLA conjugates), and a reduction of the molecular weight of some PLA chains (Fig. 1). It is possible to hypothesize that, as reported for mixture of polyesters and polyols, the extrusion process of the PLA@CDs blend provides the required energy to establish covalent interactions between PLA chains and CDs, thus leading to a mixture of CDs-PLA entangled into PLA chains with different molecular weight dispersion.

These evidences are supported by SEC traces reported in Fig. 2c. While the plain PLA consists of a bimodal molecular weight distribution (
M
¯
w 1
= 154000, PD = 1.84), after the extrusion processes appear three distinct and narrower peaks ascribable to the CDs-PLA conjugate, the main PLA matrix, and the cleaved PLA chains (
M
¯
w
: greater than 500000, 168,000 and 87000, respectively). It is noteworthy that, according to the reaction scheme proposed in Fig. 1, changes observed in the PLA molecular weight distribution provide clear evidence of the transesterification process occurred during the extrusion of the PLA@CDs blend.

With the aim of confirming the structure of the CDs-PLA conjugate, ePLA@CDs was purified by GPC, using chloroform as eluant, yielding to a colorless fraction of pure PLA and a brownish fraction containing fluorescent CDs-PLA conjugates. Then, 1H NMR spectroscopy of the retrieved CDs-PLA conjugates was performed and reported in Fig. 2d. The 1H NMR spectrum shows typical peaks of PLA with a doublet at 1.56–1.59 ppm relative to CH3 protons, and a quadruplet at 5.12–5.19 ppm corresponding to the CH proton. This evidence conclusively confirmed the effective surface functionalization of CDs with PLA chains, in agreement to FTIR and SEC data. Besides, coupling XPS and STEM analyses, we corroborated these data displaying that purified CDs-PLA conjugates obtained after melt extrusion and column purification consist of a CDs core of 5 nm completely covered by a covalently-linked PLA shell of about 36 nm (Figures S4 and S5). This implies that CDs are directly linked to PLA chains of about 80 kDa, in agreements to SEC traces.

Therefore, the one-pot synthesis of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite by heterophase melt-extrusion transesterification allows imparting to the biodegradable matrix of PLA brilliant optical properties due to crystalline N,S-CDs. Overall, the known appealing properties of PLA as one of the elected biomaterial for the production of medical devices were successfully combined with the self-tracking optical properties of CDs, to give smart biomaterials for a huge biomedical and industrial applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of ePLA@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the thermoplastic behavior of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite, DSC/TGA analysis was performed within a temperature range of 20–600 °C and a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. Results were compared to those obtained for plain PLA and CDs. As shown in Fig. 3 a, no significant weight loss and thermal transitions were revealed before 160 °C in CDs thermogram demonstrating their remarkable thermal stability. Broad endothermal transitions were observed only in the range 160–400 °C, followed by a marked decomposition up to 600 °C. In fact, the CDs’ thermogram reported in Fig. 3a’’ displays a weight loss of 41% w/w up to 600 °C, attributable to the decomposition of surface functional groups of CDs. Indeed, such a degree of thermal stability is quite unusual for CDs and organic fluorescent probes, and makes them a perfect filler candidate for extrusion process at high temperature, without incurring thermo-induced degradation phenomena and consequent photobleaching [31]. For the ePLA@CDs, the thermogram was similar to that observed for PLA but with considerable differences in term of melting temperature (Tm) and decomposition temperature (Td) (Fig. 3a-a’’). Indeed, as PLA possess a sharp Tm at 170 °C and an endothermal Td at about 356 °C, for the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite there are two endothermal transitions at 167 °C and 176 °C (Fig. 3a), ascribed to the two different PLA distributions, together with a higher Td at about 363 °C (Fig. 3a’’). The thermogravimetric curves observed for the ePLA@CDs and the plain PLA reported in displayed a comparable weight loss (97.54 and 94.56% w/w up to 378 °C, respectively), owing to the degradation of main PLA chains and rapid decrease in molecular weight at this temperature condition [32]. The different thermal stability profile of the nanocomposite demonstrates the actual covalent surface functionalization of CDs with PLA chains, homogeneously embedded in a PLA matrix. It is also important to notice that ePLA@CDs has a Tg at about 57 °C, suitable for 3D printing applications (Fig. 3 a’) [33]. The glass transition temperature is especially useful in 3D printing to find a reliable bed temperature, since the Tg of a filament should be the bed temperature that one print the filament with, thus granting enough heat to soften up for better surface and layer-by-layer adhesion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and microstructure characterization of ePLA@CDs filaments for 3D printing
ce:section.ce:para: According to the DSC/TGA profile observed for the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite, the latter was extruded at a temperature of 185 °C without undergoing thermal decomposition of all components. Premixed chops of the PLA@CDs composite obtained from solvent casting of a colloidal dispersion of CDs in a solution of PLA in DCM were loaded in the extruder’s hopper. Thus, CDs were surrounded by PLA at a quasi-molecular level, and were able to promptly react with PLA chains under mechanical and thermal stress. The chosen extrusion conditions, not only favored the transesterification process between PLA chains and CDs, but also allows obtaining perfectly homogeneous ePLA@CDs filaments of 1.75 ± 0.5 mm, as demonstrated by the micro-CT images depicted in Fig. 3b-b’’’. As shown by micro-CT sections of the filament, no aggregates of nanoparticles, segregate structures, fractures of enervations were observed, proving that melt-extrusion transesterification if a simple and industrially scalable method to prepare uniform filaments suitable for 3D printing applications.

Water wettability of ePLA@CDs and PLA solvent-cast films was evaluated by contact angle measurements in order to assess how the incorporation of N,S-CDs into PLA matrix could affect its surface properties. Although carbon nanodots were characterized by a rich content of polar groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amide groups, their incorporation within the PLA matrix did not induce an increment of hydrophilic surface. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, both samples displayed comparable contact angle, 92° against 93°, associated with a moderate hydrophobic surface. The unchanged contact angle of the ePLA@CDs confirms the formation of PLA-coated CDs dispersed throughout the bulk of the PLA matrix, which prevents the exposure of naked hydrophilic CDs on the outer surface.

Taking into account that crystalline CDs are characterized by a higher percentage of sp2 domains compared to amorphous CDs, they suffer more from self-quenching phenomena induced by surface interactions at short distance in solid-state [34]. However, surface passivation with PLA chains should ensure excellent emission profile of CDs also in solid state due to the presence of immobilized polymer chains at the CDs surface and the extremely good dispersion in the PLA solid matrix, which drastically reduce the π-π core interactions or aggregation phenomena [35]. In Fig. 4 a the absorption spectrum (OA) of bare CDs is shown with normalized emission spectra (PL) obtained at various excitation wavelengths. The OA spectrum displays a series of different electronic transitions spanning in all the visible range probably associated to a family of heterogeneous surface states featuring high radiative rates. Exciting in the visible range, the typical CDs emission tunability is shown. When CDs are embedded in PLA matrix, they keep their optical properties with some differences. On one hand, they still display similar emission tunability as shown in Fig. 4b. Comparing PL spectra of bare CDs and the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite excited at the same excitation wavelength, a huge blueshift of the emission (Fig. 4c) is evident, accompanied by variations of the band shape when the CDs are embedded in PLA matrix. As shown for other CDs, these CDs are very sensitive to different solvents (Figure S1) [36–38]. Their emission blueshifts when the polarity of the solvent decreases (from water to acetone, as shown in Figure S1d). Being PLA non-polar, its interaction with CDs surface is expected to shifts the PL band in a bluer spectral region. Therefore, changes in the observed emission provide another proof of the strong covalent interactions between CDs and PLA. Despite this modification the emission quantum yield undergoes very little variations and substantially remains unaltered (≅1% in the red) in all the synthesis phases (bare CDs, PLA@CDs and ePLA@CDs composites). In this sense, the basic optical response of CDs, that is their bright and tunable emission, is fundamentally preserved in ePLA@CDs, making the latter a suitable candidate for a variety of solid-state applications.

We also performed time resolved nanosecond spectroscopic measurements in order to evaluate differences in the emission lifetime. Inserting CDs in the PLA causes an increase on the nanosecond lifetime from 5.5 ns to 6.2 as shown in Fig. 4d, which is another evidence of strong covalent bonds between CDs and PLA chains. We also evaluated the photostability of PLA@CDs nanocomposite finding out that the composites is very stable and the emission intensity does not change if the sample is irradiated by pulsed laser (1.5 mJ, 420 nm, 10 Hz) for 3 h. This characteristic is very useful in several applications as it guarantees the non-photo-degradability of the nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation study of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Degradation phenomena usually occur for bulk PLA in physiological conditions. However, despite some changes in the microstructure, PLA seems to be largely resistant to hydrolysis and thus PLA implants (e.g., orthopedic plates and screws) persist for a long time in vivo [39,40]. PLA nanocomposites with tunable stiffness and degradation rate are desired for many medical applications.

In a physiological environment, PLA is mainly degraded by hydrolysis, and the degradation process can be divided into two stages: i) the ester bond of the PLA chain breakages and water molecules begin to penetrate PLA matrix causing the molecular weight decrease and the release of water-soluble oligomers; ii) oligomers are gradually hydrolyzed into monomeric lactic acids which catalyze the hydrolysis [41]. Thus water uptake, at least in the first step, can be used as an indirect measure of degradation rates [42]. Here, water uptake of ePLA@CDs filaments was evaluated in PBS pH 7.4 at 37 °C by monitoring samples weight every week for more than 7 months. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, bare PLA filaments display a feeble water uptake until 31 weeks, reaching a maximum of about 1%, suggesting a very low degradation rate. While, ePLA@CDs filaments gradually uptake aqueous medium (about 1.5% in 12 weeks) during the first degradation step, showing an exponential water uptake increase after 16 weeks. Water uptake reached a maximum (≈ 5.2%) after 24 weeks, and underwent a sharp decrease up to 2.1% after 31 weeks, implying that PLA oligomers hydrolyzed into monomeric lactic acids provoking damages in the filament microstructure and aqueous medium drainage towards external medium. These remarkable differences could be ascribed to the presence of CDs homogeneously embedded into the hydrophobic PLA matrix, which favor the diffusion of water between polymer chains. To study filaments microstructure changes during degradation, X-ray micro-CT analyses were performed after seven months incubation in PBS pH 7.4 and 37 °C. As expected, the PLA filaments’ microstructure appeared regular (Fig. 5b-b’) and identical to that observed for the untreated samples (Fig. 3b-b’’’), corroborating the limited degradation revealed by water uptake data. On the contrary, the microstructure observed for the ePLA@CDs filaments after the same incubation time seemed irregular and fragmented, typical signals of strong hydrolytic degradation processes (Fig. 5c-c’’’).

In particular, X-ray micro-CT analysis of degraded ePLA@CDs highlighted the presence of a major rift along the filament (Fig. 5c) and several capillary rifts within the core, as evidenced by the section reported in Fig. 5c’-c’’’. Therefore, it is possible to hypothesize that the hydrophilic character of CDs surface polar groups (–COOH, –CONH2, –NH2) favored an initial moderate water uptake and subsequent hydrolysis of the PLA chains, thus promoting formation of capillary defects able to provoke further water uptake and osmotic fractures. Therefore, the use of hydrophilic CDs as filler of PLA allows tuning the degradation rate in physiological condition, avoiding longtime degradation rates usually observed for pure PLA, which is not desired in some medical applications [28]. For example, ePLA@CDs are very appealing for bone and hard tissue engineering, where the need to finely control the degradation rate is crucial to promote a gradual substitution of the material with new functional living tissues [42]. Indeed, PLA exhibits a very slow degradation rate, hindering the regeneration of new tissues.

Considering the potential use of ePLA@CDs as filaments to produce self-tacking biomaterials by 3D printing, a very interesting and important aspect to take into account is the possibility of monitoring degradation and allocation by non-invasive fluorescence imaging (FL), made possible by the strong emissivity of the composite. Thus, we also attempted to monitor microstructure defects owing to degradation by fluorescence microscopy. Surprisingly, FL was much more informative than X-ray micro-CT analysis displaying detailed defects in the filament microstructure in a multicolor fashion (Fig. 5e-e’’’ and d-d’’’). In particular, ePLA@CDs filaments perform well as multicolor emitter (from blue to red) and their optical properties permit to efficiently tracking morphological changes on surface as well as the formation of capillary rifts into the core over time. In addition, a denser filament core can be promptly visible, suggesting a different degradation rate passing from the surface to the filament core (Fig. 5 d-d’’’). The brilliant multicolor fluorescence in solid-state and the excellent photo-stability make the proposed multifunctional nanocomposite a promising degradable biomaterial suitable for the production of self-tracking medical devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of cell adhesion and proliferation on PLA@CDs films
ce:section.ce:para: Cell adhesion is a fundamental requirement to promote cell survival and proliferation in tissue engineering applications. It is considered the first step for successive cell diffusion, migration, proliferation, and differentiation, which is of fundamental importance for the clinical success of a biomaterial [43]. Considering the plethora of possible medical application of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite, we evaluated the material-cell interaction on ePLA@CDs films of two in vitro cell models, MC3T3 and NHDF, on ePLA@CDs films, and results were compared to those obtained for plain PLA films used as control. Cell adhesion evaluated in term of cell viability was evaluated by MTS assay after 24 h and 48 h from the incubation.

As show in Fig. 6 , after 24 h of incubation it was observed that cell adhesion of both cell lines was more marked on PLA@CDs films in comparison with plain PLA films, showing an Abs492 24h value two times higher. The ability of a material to interact with cells promoting their adhesion and proliferation is commonly ascribed to surface wettability, although different studies demonstrated that other factors such as serum protein adsorption, surface potential, and surface morphology influence these phenomena [44,45]. Indeed, as above reported, CDs do not impinge on the contact angle of PLA films, showing a moderate hydrophobic surface. On the other hand, SEM analysis highlighted that ePLA@CDs films were characterized by a higher and homogeneous micro-roughness than plain PLA films (Figure S6a-a’), suggesting a key role of roughness in inducing cell adhesion and proliferation of cells [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3-D printing of self-tracking biomaterials: A proof-of-concept study
ce:section.ce:para: Printability of ePLA@CDs filaments was evaluated by an additive manufacturing 3D printing technique, building up objects with increasing architectural complexity such as: a circle grid (Fig. 7 a), a dodecagon (Fig. 7a’-a’’) and a screw (insert of Fig. 7b). The 3D printed models were characterized by a well-defined structure, by fine tuning printing parameters such as temperature, speed, nozzle diameter and nozzle-substrate distance, and extruded filament behavior. As we can see in detail in Fig. 7a’’, it was obtained a regular deposition of interconnected ePLA@CDs filaments, perfectly aligned and distinguishable, which proves the good processability of the ePLA@CDs nanocomposite, and its potential use for 3D additive manufacturing applications. The ePLA@CDs nanocomposite was also suitable to produce complex objects such as orthopedic screws (Fig. 7b). Their macroscopic features, evaluated by X-ray micro-CT analysis, indeed showed a regular layer-by-layer deposition of ePLA@CDs with a homogeneous matrix due to a good adhesion of extruded filaments (Fig. 7b-b’). It is noteworthy that the peculiar brightness and multicolor emission features of the embedded CDs is preserved after 3D printing (Fig. 7c), paving the way to a huge range of industrial and medical applications such as in anticounterfeiting technology, orthopedic implant surgery and self-tracking medical devices.

Herein, we designed for the first time a cost-effective and industrially scalable one-pot synthesis of fluorescent biodegradable and biocompatible CDs@PLA nanocomposites by heterophase melt-extrusion transesterification process, exploiting CDs as polar multicolor fluorescent probe filler and PLA as well-known biocompatible thermoplastic matrix. We first demonstrated that heterophase melt-extrusion transesterification between hydroxyl groups at the CDs surface and PLA ester bonds is a valid strategy to obtain PLA-coated CDs homogenously embedded in a PLA matrix (ePLA/CDs). As demonstrated by DSC/TGA data, the conjugation of CDs with PLA chains, and the consequent formation of a nanocomposite material, confers to the ePLA/CDs nanocomposite superior thermal stability without perturbing the typical glass transition temperature of plain PLA. This implies that ePLA/CDs is a suitable blend for additive manufacturing and 3D printing applications. Indeed, we demonstrated that ePLA/CDs filaments of 1.7 mm can be easily produced and employed to print a wide selection of objects with increasing complexity, such as orthopedic screws, endowed with high morphological and structural homogeneity, and typical fluorescence characteristics. In fact, the use of CDs as probe-filler, due to their high thermal and optical stability and lack of self-quenching thanks to the incorporation strategy, provided stable multicolor emission to printed biomaterials. We also demonstrated that the CDs filler enhances PLA degradation rate and in vitro cell adhesion and proliferation, desired features in tissue engineering applications. Overall, the proposed strategy represents a successful and innovative use of CDs as functional probe-filler to simultaneously tune degradation of polyesters, to provide self-tracking abilities by multicolor fluorescence imaging, and to promote tissues repair. Results make ePLA@CDs an appealing printable biomaterial exploitable in the production of a range of prospective smart biomedical devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We must really thank Mr. Paolo Bonomo, Advanced Technology Network (ATeN) Center – University of Palermo, Ed. 18, Palermo (Italy), for the micro-CT measures. Authors have no financial/commercial conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.136525.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
